item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article was taken by author for the article, and as such is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the fuck… i-is this… the origin of dado? Bad english, false advertising…. the similarities are stunning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa…..I love, just absolutely love, the fact that thing thing has exponential danger. For every one that breaches containment, every other one becomes worse. And so on and so forth until it’s an unstoppable force. My god that is terrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a second…
Extreme heat kills it and high pressure stops it from opening in the first place?
If only SCP-1543-j wasn't a -j.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Wow, this one is pretty out there. Rather than trying to reinvent the wheel, I see a nice mention to the GOC that could be turned into a link to that GoI's hub, which in turn would open up that plethora of material to the reader. Granted, if any of our esteemed readers have additional crosslinks to suggest, hit me up. I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

The "Global Occult Coalition" group of interest will report any movement or activity from the containment areas they administrate to contain SCP-1583 instances. If the possibility arises to seize an instance from one of these locations, it should be acted upon immediately. Currently, the Foundation and GOC have contained 61 instances of SCP-1583.

To this:

The "Global Occult Coalition" group of interest will report any movement or activity from the containment areas they administrate to contain SCP-1583 instances. If the possibility arises to seize an instance from one of these locations, it should be acted upon immediately. Currently, the Foundation and GOC have contained 61 instances of SCP-1583.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is something about iffy english that conveys a lot of scary.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or, the pressure at roughly 1.4 km deep in the ocean. Which is about twice the crush depth of even the most advanced submarines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternate explanation: 14 MPa is, if I'm correct, 14 MegaPascals, which is 14,000,000 Pascals, which is 14,000,000 Newtons per meter squared. Holy shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All i can think when i read the name is "your resistance only makes me harder"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: considering the last post concerning proofreading was last year and its still a huge mess.. i have to downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "SCP-1583" isn't so much misleading as it is imprecise. "SCP-1583.0" would have been better. Gotta remember sig figs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How was the title misleading?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Article is good, title is misleading though

Abstain on voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rioghail mentioned it, actually, but the "possible to destroy" line is the major throw for me.

+1 for the concept (I like tentacle monsters, alright?), but it needs some proofing and revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article needs better proofreading. There are several examples of rather suspect sentence construction and a few more obvious instances of just plain bad grammar. "The instances are possible to destroy" in particular leaped out and smacked me around the face.

I'd advise combing through and maybe reading the article aloud to check for errors like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I see the difficulty in containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thoroughly meh on the new revisions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
140 atm, if I'm remembering right.
Which … is quite bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this needs to be Keter. It's easy to contain as long as you hold it shut. I'm not sure how much 14 MPa is but it doesn't sound too bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My mental picture had them under some giant hydraulic piston in some reinforced chamber somewhere. A "boot" for the can seems it would be insufficient for anticipated future breaches.

But that's kinda the problem. The containment procedures don't actually specify how is it contained - saying "held down by 14 MPa", is vague and meaningless from the perspective of what's being done, nevermind that a value with an unit without defining what is it a measure of is horrid tone.
Nevermind that encasing it tightly would work better, and the fact that the internal pressure (if it's 14MPa) is already enough to go over yield stress of aluminium - I'm just taking that they're fancy anomalous barrels made of something excessively strong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to share with you a couple my thoughts that seem pertinent to questions you raise:

I would think that anything that would keep the lid on the cannister would have to be attached to the can itself, rather than being part of a room.

My mental picture had them under some giant hydraulic piston in some reinforced chamber somewhere. A "boot" for the can seems it would be insufficient for anticipated future breaches.

Then how was anyone able to get the cannisters while they were still shut? Is there some specific external event that triggers this? Or does it just spontaneously happen at random?

Just work backwards: As more cans are opened, the internal pressure of the other cans increases. So when fewer cans were open, the internal pressure was less. When no cans were open, the internal pressure was probably low enough to be held back by the lids alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All chambers are fitted with devices preventing the SCP-1583 instances lids from opening.

1) This is kind of vague.

2) I would think that anything that would keep the lid on the cannister would have to be attached to the can itself, rather than being part of a room.

All instances of SCP-1583 must be held down with at least 14 MPa being pressed at all times.

Again, this seems too vague.

Outer epidermis will be destroyed within 14 seconds of contact, followed by muscles and other tissue. The subject's skeletal structure will slowly dissolve over a period of 2-3 hours.

Have the organisms ever tried to reconstruct anything they absorbed?

This organism is capable of opening SCP-1583 on its own, if there is nothing preventing it from exiting on the other side.

Then how was anyone able to get the cannisters while they were still shut? Is there some specific external event that triggers this? Or does it just spontaneously happen at random?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is on the right track. I rather like "Department of Defend", and I'm assuming it's some knockoff organization that tries to pass itself off as the actual DoD. Agreeing that we need to know there are more than one of these, as it wasn't clear from the outset. Some smoothing out of the article is in order as well; see above suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this might work better as a GOC article, since destroying them IS apparently the best solution. Or, at the very least, have there be some sign that the Foundation is actually trying to coordinate with the GOC on this, because keter buckets are pretty serious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, now talking from the side of the Coalition. Why don't they use a massive H.I.T bomb to destroy all the instances they posses at the same time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also also, the connection to the fallout shelters isn't really clear. I expected the entities to tear people apart so that they can reassemble them after the war is over and the radiation levels are low enough, with hook being in the imperfections of the reassembling process; but this isn't hinted at all in the article, so it's likely this wasn't author's intention. Which leaves me puzzled about the shelters.

This is what I was expecting too, and would have been awesome. As it is now, Engrish-speaking wormy things that advertise falsely aren't that interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the last note implies that the GOC think they have them all, and will try to destroy them simultaneously, at which point those that are contained by the Foundation will go boom. Am I getting this right? Why can't this be solved by just warning GOC, seeing as it's one of the few groups the Foundation is on speaking terms with?

Also, apart from some awkward sentence structure, the designation is confusing, referring both to the buckets and to the entities that come out from the buckets.

Also also, the connection to the fallout shelters isn't really clear. I expected the entities to tear people apart so that they can reassemble them after the war is over and the radiation levels are low enough, with hook being in the imperfections of the reassembling process; but this isn't hinted at all in the article, so it's likely this wasn't author's intention. Which leaves me puzzled about the shelters.

I don't know, I like bits of this. I'll no-vote for now, but I might revote later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's also the thing about how aluminium has a finite yield strength - there's only so many of them that can be breached before they all go boom, and holding the lid on top won't do shit to help.

Which makes me think, has anyone tried to encase them in something? Why not?

EDIT:

held down with at least 14 MPa being pressed.

Bast. There *has* to be an engrish meta effect running on this one.
That said, it doesn't impress me either, since it's not at all meaningfully connected to the rest of the entry, asides from the note being engrish too.

Alternately, Roget is trying to pull Sokal shit on us.

EDIT3: Fixed the citation. While it is slightly less awful than what I misread, it's still vague to the point of being meaningless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this first needs to be very clear exactly how many there are (or that the Foundation knows of), and how many the Foundation has, and how many everyone else has. Second, while I understand how the cumulative effects of breaching various instances is related to the message/purpose of it, but that is not the strong point of the article, and yet that appears to be the main focus of the article. I'm personally most interested in what happens when they come out to rebuild the world, and was disappointed that it wasn't addressed even a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this would be a stronger article if you focused on the note and its implications as a hook, not so much the "but we can't really contain it bc GoC" bit.

Edit: Revised, much improved, have an upvote. Interview logs might be interesting to illustrate how reconstruction affects people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it enough to upvote it now, but VAElynx is right. There are some issues with sentence structure at various places in the document that need addressing. Will itemize on request.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh.
The article itself isn't particularly grabbing, and the language of the entry is confusing and looking like it hasn't been proofread at all. EDIT: And I didn't mean the DoD note.

EDIT2: Unless of course this is one of those articles where the engrish is a meta effect on the document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So they're buckets, with writing in Engrish enticing people to open them, and if they do an interdimensional tentacle monster pokes its wriggly appendages out and eats them.

Hmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmm.

I think I like this.
